Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

(The Qur'an is the greatest mercy of Allah). The Most Merciful, Taught the Qur'an. (1-2) That is, it is the special favor of Allah that He sent down the Qur'an as a mercy and blessing for your teaching and reminding. If He should will, He could have sent down the punishment according to your demand; but due to His extreme mercy, He blessed you with His scripture of mercy so that you study it, understand it, and in its light, correct your intellectual and practical crookedness to flourish in this world and achieve success in the Hereafter. The meaning is: when your Merciful Lord has blessed you with His mercy, why do you seek His punishment instead of His blessing!

"Created man, Taught him eloquence." (3-4)

(Human nature required that the Qur'an was revealed for his guidance). That is, along with the requirements of Allah's mercy, the creation of man and his attributes also required that the scripture of guidance was sent down for his guidance instead of the whip of punishment. Allah Almighty created man and taught him speech. This speech is a testimony to the fact that the Creator has made him an intelligent and conscious being. He is capable of hearing and understanding, can distinguish between good and evil, and can deliver and explain it to others. When he is adorned with these various capabilities, it clearly means that nature willed that he is taught through speech instead of being driven like animals. It should be kept in mind that the capability of speech within man is a testimony to his other various capabilities. This speech requires that man is intelligent and conscious, can deduce particulars from universals and universals from particulars, and possesses the capability of reasoning, derivation, and judgment. For this reason, Aristotle declared this attribute of man to be what distinguishes him from animals. If this attribute is absent, he is not human, but an animal walking on two legs; and from here, it also emerged that those who are waiting to see a reality with their eyes instead of understanding it in the light of proofs are also included in the category of animals, even if they live in magnificent bungalows.

"The sun and the moon [run] by formula, And the stars and the trees prostrate." (5, 6)

(A pointer to the signs of the universe). This draws attention to the signs of the universe: if you seek signs for the confirmation of the Prophet's warning, why do you await a new sign? Look at the signs of the sky above you, how this sun and moon are revolving in their orbits with full discipline and formula. It is impossible for a difference of a minute or second to occur. Then see how they observe those limits and boundaries which Allah Almighty has appointed for them; it never happens that the sun exceeds its boundary to enter the orbit of the moon, or the moon enters the boundaries of the sun. 'It is not allowable for the sun to reach the moon, nor does the night outrun the day, but each, in an orbit, is swimming.' (Ya-Sin: 40). Does this sign not teach humans every day that when the Creator of this universe has bound great creations like the sun and the moon to His limits and boundaries, why would He leave humans free of His commands and prohibitions? and if humans dare to create corruption in the world by breaking His limits, why would He not punish them? The one who has implemented His law in every corner of the universe, his following is most deserving for humans.

"And the stars and the trees prostrate." After referring to the sun and the moon's observing of limits—which in the terminology of the Shariah is described as 'piety'—this mentions the prostration of the stars of the sky and the trees of the earth: they also prostrate to their Creator and Owner, and by their actions, invite humans not to rebel against their Lord, but to prostrate and worship Him with obedience.

The prostration of the stars and the trees has been explained in various places. See this discussion in its proper place.

(The correct meaning of 'Al-Najm'). By 'Al-Najm', some have meant the small plants, bushes, and creepers growing on the earth. Probably, the connection of the stars with the trees was not understood by these scholars; because of this, they had to resort to this affectation, whereas there is a highly clear common attribute between the two. In the Qur'an, the mention of their prostration has occurred in various styles. Due to this commonality, the mention of both occurred together here. This clarifies the harmony of both the heavens and the earth—showing that their Lord is One, Whom the stars of the sky also prostrate to and the trees of the earth also. It should be kept in mind that Mujahid (ra), Qatadah (ra), and Hasan (ra) took 'Al-Najm' in its well-known meaning (the stars). Ibn Kathir (ra) also supported them and quoted: 'Do you not see that to Allah prostrates whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth and the sun, the moon, the stars...' (Al-Hajj: 18).

"And the heaven He raised and imposed the balance," (7)

After drawing attention to some bright signs of the sky, He drew attention to the sky itself—stating: look at it, how your Lord raised this vast canopy without pillars, whose vastness no one can estimate, then see how, despite this vastness and greatness, He maintained such balance in it that no one can find any defect or rift in any corner of it. In another place, He stated:

'He created the heavens without pillars that you see and has cast into the earth firmly set mountains, lest it shift with you...' (Luqman: 10). In Surah Al-Mulk, He stated: '[And] He who created seven heavens in layers. You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any inconsistency. So return [your] vision; do you see any breaks? Then return [your] vision twice again. [Your] vision will return to you humbled and fatigued.' (Al-Mulk: 3-4).

From 'without pillars that you see' (bighayri 'amadin tarawnaha), it is learned that to maintain the balance of the sky, Allah Almighty used those invisible pillars of gravitational pull which are not seen by others.

"That you not transgress within the balance." (8)

(The Creator's pleasure is obvious from His attribute). That is, when the Creator placed the balance in it upon which it is established, without which the sky would be disrupted, His desire and His pleasure was learned: He desires that man also maintains balance, justice, and equity within his sphere of choice. He must not create any corruption in this balance, otherwise corruption will spread in the entire system of livelihood. The meaning is: this very justice and equity is what the Qur'an invites you to, whose testimony is present in every corner of the sky above you, and of whose violation He is warning you: if you, in the blindness of your rebellion, disrupt this balance, you will face its consequences in this world and in the Hereafter. So why do you not understand this obvious truth, whose testimony is present in every corner of the heavens and the earth! Why are you anxious for the whip of punishment, ignoring these signs of warning!

"And establish weight in justice and do not make deficient the balance." (9)

The preceding statement was a general rule. This points to another reality based on this rule, which relates to our daily life. It states: when the Creator of the sky under which you live is the One Who placed the balance and loves justice, do not lead a life of dishonesty in His earth, but establish weight in justice and never make the balance deficient. Under the story of the people of Shu'ayb, we pointed to the reality: making the balance deficient is no individual evil, but is a terrifying sign of corruption in the entire social system. Here, an additional reality is clarified: this evil is contrary to that balance upon which Allah Almighty has established the heavens and the earth. If any nation accepts this corruption, it means they are bent upon destroying the very foundation upon which Allah Almighty has built this world. Obviously, Allah Almighty can never tolerate such people in His earth.

Here, it is also noteworthy that the same command is stated in both positive and negative styles. In the Qur'an, this style is chosen in those places where violating the command leads to highly dangerous results. This shows that the command of perfect justice in balance is a great command. It is, in its reality, a branch of that balance of justice upon which Allah Almighty has established the system of this world, and from here, it also emerged that the nation which creates corruption in this, creates corruption in the entire social system.

"And the earth He laid [down] for the creatures. Therein is fruit and palm trees having sheaths [of dates] And grain having husks and scented plants." (10-13)

(Pointing to the provisions of lordship in the earth). After drawing attention to the wonders of the sky, He drew attention to the provisions of lordship in the earth. For the sky, the word 'He raised' (rafa'aha) was used, and in contrast, for the earth, 'He laid [down]' (wada'aha) is highly suitable and meaningful—stating: He raised the sky like a canopy, and laid down the earth like a bed, so that it became a comfortable home for His creations. Then, just as He placed the sun, moon, and stars as lamps in the sky to provide light and heat, He placed various kinds of fruits, grains, and flowers in this home to provide food, to make them enjoy, and to provide comfort for their eyes and minds.

Here, the mention of flowers (scented plants) along with grains and fruits points to the reality that Allah Almighty has not only arranged for His servants to fill their stomachs, but has also arranged for their pleasure, beauty, and comfort, which is proof of His care in lordship. Similarly, 'having husks' (dhu-l-asf) with 'grain' (Al-Habb), and 'having sheaths' (dhati-l-akmam) with 'palm trees' (Al-Nakhl), are proofs of His special favor—showing: He gave grains and fruits not as if He threw them, but He made such an excellent arrangement of packaging for every single grain and fruit that man is amazed on seeing it. Obviously, this arrangement was made so that man recognizes the right of His lordship, remains grateful to Him, and lives keeping always before him the reality: He Who made this entire arrangement for him without any entitlement will not leave him without purpose, but He will also bring a Day of Account. Regarding the word 'Rayhan' (scented plants), a clarification is also necessary: some have interpreted it as leaves, but this word has not occurred in this meaning in Arabic, nor is there any context to mention leaves along with grains. It seems that the mention of flowers along with grains appeared out of place to these scholars; because of this, they had to resort to this unique interpretation, whereas there is a context for it which we have explained.

"So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (13)

(A specific reason for addressing the Jinn). This verse will occur repeatedly below, and is the most important verse of this Surah; because of this, in the preface, we have explained the analysis of the word 'Ala'' (favors), and also highlighted the nature of addressing both the Jinn and humans. However, one point we will clarify is: conjoining the deniers among the Jinn with the deniers among the Quraysh here has a specific reason—that in this period, the campaign of denial had become highly intense, and the devils of Jinn and men combined to try to defeat the truth. For this reason, Allah Almighty addressed both directly to rebuke them, and the subsequent verses will show that He challenged them: let them try their best, His word will be elevated against them. In the preface, we explained that this Surah was revealed in that period of the Meccan life when the Quraysh were caught in extreme stubbornness. In this period, obviously, they must have received the greatest support from the devils among the Jinn. This required that both were addressed.

(Gratitude to the Benefactor is the Requirement of Nature). Gratitude to the Benefactor upon receiving His blessings, and the feeling of responsibility under His lordship, are self-evident requirements of human nature. If a person is benefiting from His blessings, but does not accept that His right is established upon him, or that he will be questioned regarding His blessings, it means he is denying all these blessings. The Qur'an has rebuked the Quraysh and their companion Jinn for this denial—showing: the blessings of your Lord are present before you in every way to remind you of the Day of Account, yet you are denying them, so which of the blessings and favors of your Lord would you deny!

"He created man from clay like [that of] pottery. And He created the jinn from a smokeless flame of fire. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (14-16)

(Arguing for the Resurrection from the Stages of Human Creation). This reminds both Jinn and humans of their creation to draw attention to their second creation—showing: do not be in the misconception that after creating you once, your Lord is no longer competent to recreate you, because of which the matter of the Day of Account is out of possibility. Remember that the material from which He created you is present, as is His power of creation, just as they were present at the time of your first creation; so if you cannot deny your first creation, you have no proof to deny your recreation. 'Salsal' refers to pure clay, and 'Fakh-khar' is that clay which becomes dry like pottery. The stages through which life passes to reach the human stage have occurred in various places. In some places He stated: He created man from water, in some He referred to clay, in some He mentioned altered clay. Here, dry clay is mentioned. Similarly, the final stage is mentioned as: He ran his progeny from a sperm-drop. These are the evolutionary stages of human life, whose explanation has passed under verse 26 of Surah Al-Hijr. The objective of describing these stages is to make man observe His power, wisdom, and lordship within his own existence—so that the reality is clarified: it is His glory alone that He extracted him from water and clay, nurtured him in the dry and moderate climate, gradually blessed him with a new form, and established a new system to run his progeny. Describing this is to prove that the existence of man is not without purpose, and also to show: the Wise, Knowing God Who can make a cell born in clay into a human, nothing is far from His power, He is competent over everything.

(The Jinn were created from a flame of fire). 'And He created the jinn from a smokeless flame of fire' (Wa-khalaqal-janna min marijim-min-nar)—'Marij' means a flame. The flame is the essence of fire. Just as man was created from the essence and kernel of clay, the Jinn were created from the kernel of fire. The details of their stages of life are not described in the Qur'an, because it is difficult for us to understand the details of their creation. After this is that verse of repetition whose explanation has passed. Its meaning here is: when you observe these glories, powers, and wisdoms of your Lord within your own existence, why do you consider being raised alive again for the Day of Account improbable, and how long will you deny His signs and demand new signs!

"[He is] Lord of the two easts and Lord of the two wests. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (17-18)

(The reason for bringing the dual form of the east and the west). That is: He alone is the Lord of the east and the west. No one is outside His boundaries, nor is any other partner in His divinity. Allah is the Sovereign of the entire east and west without any partner. Not even a small area in His heavens and earth belongs to anyone else, such that they could save its people from His grasp. In Surah Al-Ma'arij, this is expressed as: 'So I swear by the Lord of the sunrises and sunsets that indeed We are competent To replace them with better than they; and We are not to be outrun.' (40-41). The dual form of 'Mashriqayn' (the two easts) and 'Maghribayn' (the two wests) has been generally explained as the east and west of winter and summer, but this is an affectation. In the Qur'an, these words are used in the singular, dual, and plural, and in all three forms, there is no difference in meaning. In the dual, the objective is to encompass both ends; and in the plural, to point to the infinite vastness of the horizons. Examples of such usages have passed. At the end is that verse of repetition; its meaning here is: when His majesty is such that the east and the west are under His control, if you assume that His warning is an empty threat, which of His majesties would you deny!

"He released the two seas, meeting [side by side]; Between them is a barrier [so] neither of them transgresses. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (19-21)

(Proof of Tawhid in terms of the harmony among opposites). This presents proof of Tawhid in terms of the harmony among the opposites in this universe. The meaning is: the apparent opposition in every corner of the universe—rising and setting, day and night, cold and heat—should not lead anyone to the misconception that different wills are operating within it. If a person reflects, he will find a highly deep harmony and compatibility in these opposites. Every opposite meets with its opposite to serve a higher purpose, which is proof: a single will is dominant over this entire universe, using all its opposites under His wisdom for its collective benefit. It states: He released the two seas, one is salty and the other sweet, they collide with one another, but the Powerful, Wise God has placed an invisible barrier between them so that they meet but each remains on its characteristics. It does not happen that the salty sea becomes sweet, or the sweet salty. This shows: those who fell into the misconception of making different deities because of the apparent opposition did not reflect on this harmony, which is the greatest proof of Tawhid. At the end is that verse of repetition; its meaning here is: if despite these clear signs, you are in the misconception that your deities will save you from His grasp, which of His signs would you deny!

"From both of them emerge pearl and coral. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (22-23) This points to a shared benefit achieved from them despite their opposition—showing that from both, pearl and coral are achieved, which are wealth and ornament for man.

(A baseless claim of some commentators). Some commentators have raised the question: pearls and corals emerge only from salty water, but the Qur'an states that they emerge from both, so what is the answer? In our view, the answer is: the claim is entirely baseless that pearls emerge only from salty water. In the *Encyclopædia Britannica*, the article on 'Pearl' contains this: 'In the northern hemisphere, freshwater mussels (Freshwater Unionidae) have produced highly valuable pearls. In the United States, freshwater pearls are harvested mostly from the Mississippi River. In Britain, the industry is now in decline, but the pearls harvested from the rivers of Scotland—the Spey and Tay—and the river Conwy in North Wales enjoyed great demand. In China, harvesting pearls from freshwater has been known since 1000 BC.'

And even if they emerged only from salty water, it does not affect the statement of the Qur'an. The law of nature is that things are created from the meeting of opposites. A child is born of the meeting of man and woman. Although she is nurtured in the womb, she is created of both. In the same way, pearls are created of the meeting of both sweet and salty waters, although they are nurtured in salty water. At the end is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

"And to Him belong the ships [with sails] raised in the sea like mountains. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (24-25)

(Another sign of harmony within opposites). This draws attention to another sign of harmony: it is of His power that massive ships like mountains sail upon the chest of the seas. Although any heavy thing sinks when put in water, His power and wisdom has made the ship, carrying thousands of tons of cargo, sail, cleaving the waves. 'To Him belong' (Wa-lahu) means that creating such compatibility is in the control of Allah alone. Only His power and wisdom can manifest such miracles, and you observe this, so which of the favors of your Lord would you deny. In Surah Fatir, this is expressed as:

'And the two seas are not alike: one is sweet and thirst-quenching, pleasant to drink, and the other is salty and bitter. And from each you eat tender meat and extract ornaments which you wear, and you see the ships cleaving through it that you may seek of His bounty and that perhaps you will be grateful.' (Fatir: 12). Under the verses of Surah Ash-Shura, this has also passed. For details, have a look at it.

"Everyone upon the earth will perish, And there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (26-28)

(The necessary consequence of the above proofs). The referent of the pronoun is 'Al-Ard' (the earth) which has been mentioned from verse 10. The arrangement of sustenance He has made for man in the earth, the glories of His creation He has shown, His power encompassing every corner, and His oneness manifest in the harmony of opposites, require that one day all these things will perish, and His Exalted, Wise Essence alone will remain, before Whom everyone must return, and He will judge everyone in accordance with their deeds, and no one will dare to open their mouth or intercede without His permission. 'Owner of Majesty and Honor' (Dhu-l-Jalali wal-Ikram) has also occurred in the final verse of this Surah. The objective of bringing these two words is to show: He alone is great in His essence; therefore, He alone is the true deserving recipient of honor. No other is His partner in these attributes. 'Face' (wajh) is used here for His essence. The face is the most honorable part; because of this, it is sometimes used to refer to the entire essence. At the end is that verse of repetition, and its meaning is: whether you listen to the Prophet's warning or do not, ultimately all will perish, and Allah's essence alone will remain, so which of His signs would you deny!

"All those within the heavens and the earth ask Him; every day He is bringing about a matter. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (29-30)

(Allah is the true point of reference). That is: He alone is the true point of reference. Whoever receives, receives His bestowing. 'Asking' (su'al) here is used in terms of its result. That is: since every asker receives from Him, from whomever he asks, in reality he asks from Him. Asking from others is entirely in vain. No other can give or take. For this reason, making anyone besides Him your protector and place of reference is sheer foolishness. Whatever man receives in the world is His gift, and whatever he receives in the Hereafter will be His gift. 'Su'al' is also used in the sense of need. (For instance, in Surah Fussilat: 'equally for those who ask' [10]). If this meaning is taken, it means everyone in the heavens and the earth is in need of Him.

(The entire management of the universe is in His control). 'every day He is bringing about a matter'—meaning do not be in the misconception that He, after creating this world, has entrusted its management to your assumed partners to sit aside as a marginalized entity. Those who are in this misconception are unaware of His glories. Allah Almighty manages His universe Himself. He moves the sun and moon in the heavens, and provides for His servants in the earth; because of this, His every moment is in some work. 'Yawm' (day) is used here in the sense of time. The meaning is: when Allah Almighty manages this world Himself, what need remains for other mediums! Then, let whoever wants to ask, ask of Him alone. At the end is that verse of repetition, and its meaning is: He is not sitting aside, but is active in the management of His world, and His glories are manifest in every corner, so which of His glories would you deny!

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 31–45

Next is the depiction of the Day of Judgment. The sequence of the discourse is entirely clear. Recite the verses. (31-45)

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"We will attend to you, O two heavy groups. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (31-32)

(The threat of reward and punishment). 'Thaqalan' refers to both Jinn and humans, as communities. Since they form heavy groups as communities, 'thaqalan' is used. The usage of this word clarifies the challenge given to both Jinn and humans simultaneously. The meaning is: no matter how massive your armies might be, Allah will soon attend to you alone. 'Attend' points to the reality: this worldly life is currently running, and His wisdom requires that it completes its term; but that time is not far when it will complete its term. At that time, Allah Almighty will attend entirely to the account of His servants and reward or punish them as they deserve. This style of expression also shows: He will perform this with complete focus. This is because this is the primary objective of creating the world. If this had been absent, this world would be reduced to a plaything. At the end is that verse of repetition; its meaning here is: you denied the proofs established in this world for reward and punishment, but what will you do when you see the Resurrection has occurred and He has established His court? Will you still deny? If so, which of His signs would you deny!

"O company of jinn and mankind, if you are able to pass beyond the regions of the heavens and the earth, then pass. You will not pass except by authority [i.e., permission]. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (33-34)

(Nothing is outside His grasp). That is: if your assumption is that you are entirely free and independent, then try to pass beyond His heavens and earth to prove that you can escape from His grasp. 'You will not pass except by authority' (la tanfudhoona illa bi-sultan)—'sultan' means authority, and also credentials. Here, it is in this second sense. That is: no matter how much you try, you cannot pass beyond the regions of the heavens and the earth. This is possible only if you have credentials (permission), and obviously, no other besides Allah Almighty can give this to you. At the end is that verse of repetition; its meaning here is: if you do not understand this proof of your helplessness, which of His powers would you deny!

"There will be sent upon you a plume of fire and [melted] copper, and you will not defend yourselves. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (35-36) That is: if anyone tries to pass beyond His boundaries, a plume of fire and melted copper will be sent upon him, and it will be so severe that no one will be able to defend himself. 'shuwadhun min nar' (plume of fire) refers to those piercing flames which are thrown upon those Jinn who try to eavesdrop at the exalted assembly. In the Qur'an, their confession of this is: 'And we have become certain that we can never escape Allah on earth, nor can we escape Him by flight.' (Al-Jinn: 12).

(A misconception of the commentators). 'Nuhas' has been generally interpreted as smoke, but this word is not well-known in this meaning. Although some lexicographers have mentioned it as a rare meaning and referred to a verse, first, that verse is subject to criticism, and second, it is difficult to understand why an unfamiliar word would be used in place of 'dukhan' (smoke) when the Qur'an was sent down in clear Arabic. For this reason, we hesitate to accept this meaning. In our view, it is used in its well-known meaning (copper), and is among those testifications of His power mentioned in 'shuwadhun min nar'. The explanation is: scientific research has shown that most meteors burn up in the atmosphere. They fall as massive metallic and rock spheres, but their kinetic energy is transformed into heat upon entering the atmosphere, which melts them into fire spheres, and their maddy is lost during their fall. However, the meteors found on the earth can be divided into three groups:

1. Metallic meteors (Siderites)
2. Rock meteors (Aerolites)
3. Rock-metallic meteors (Siderolites)

Just as iron and stone components have been found in these meteors, research has also shown copper components in them, which is mentioned in this verse as 'nuhas'. These metallic components are lost due to intense heat, but their being found in the meteors on the earth confirms the statement of the Qur'an.

"And when the heaven is split and becomes rose-colored like oil [i.e., red hide] - So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (37-38)

'Dihan' has been interpreted as oil, but this word is also well-known in the meaning of hide. Redness is compared to hide beautifully; therefore, we have preferred this meaning. In another place: 'And when the sky is stripped away' (At-Takwir: 11)—meaning: just as when the skin is stripped away, the flesh appears red, the sky will appear red. Here, its redness is compared to red hide, and this comparison is highly suitable. This is a depiction of the Day of Judgment: today you are denying, but that Day is coming when the sky will split and this canopy will appear like red hide. The meaning is: what will you do then? Will you still deny? Which of His signs would you deny, as His signs of power will continuously appear.

"So on that Day no question will be asked of man or jinn as to his sin, So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny? The criminals will be known by their marks, and they will be seized by the forelocks and the feet. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (39-42)

(The criminals' guilt will be obvious). That is: do not be in the misconception that to investigate your crimes, Allah Almighty and His angels will have to make some effort. They will not even need to ask. On that Day, every criminal will be recognized by his mark. The reason why there will be no need for questioning is described in other places—showing: on that Day, not only his hands and feet, but every single hair of his body will testify to his crimes. As for the questioning which is for reproach, sarcasm, or mockery, it has not been negated, and those questions mentioned in the Qur'an are of this second nature. There is no contradiction, but the contexts are different.

(Depiction of being cast into Hell). 'and they will be seized by the forelocks and the feet'—this is a depiction of their being cast into Hell: they will be brought with their mark of guilt, and the keepers of Hell will seize them by their hair and their feet to throw them into Hell, just as logs of wood are thrown into the fire. The mention of being cast into Hell is omitted due to contextual clarity. At the end is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear: this is the stage you must face, so which of your Lord's signs would you deny!

"This is Hell, which the criminals deny. They will go around between it and scalding water, heated [to the utmost]. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (43-45)

(Alternation between fire and boiling water). It states: this is the depiction of Hell which the criminals deny; ultimately, they will alternate between its fire and its boiling water. 'An' (heated) is used for water whose heat has reached its utmost limit. This is a depiction of their extreme helplessness: when the fire of Hell burns them, they will seek water, but the water's heat will have reached its utmost limit. Their lives will pass in alternating between them. In Surah Al-Ghashiyah, their state is depicted as: 'They will drag [them] into a burning fire. They will be given to drink from a boiling spring.' (4-5). At the end is that verse of repetition, and its context is clear: today you are denying, but what will you do when this appears! Which of His signs would you deny!

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 46–78

After describing the fate of the criminals, this describes the reward of the pious. This contrast places the complete picture before us. The sequence of the discourse is entirely clear. Just remember: verses 46–61 describe the status of the close ones, and the subsequent verses describe the Paradise of the companions of the right. The details of the difference in their ranks will follow in the subsequent Surah, Al-Waqi'ah. Recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"But for he who has feared the position of his Lord are two gardens - So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (46-47)

(The fate of the pious). After the fate of the criminals, this describes the reward of the pious. Their attribute is: they feared their standing before their Lord. That Day is mentioned in another place as: 'The Day when mankind will stand before the Lord of the worlds' (Al-Mutaffifeen: 6). It should be kept in mind: the only thing which keeps a person on the straight path is this fear of standing before his Lord. If this fear is absent, nothing can prevent him from becoming a great criminal. It states: for those who feared, there will be two gardens. 'Jannat' (garden) is a comprehensive expression for all success, and two gardens are mentioned for the completion of favor—showing that they will receive two gardens. In verse 32 of Surah Al-Kahf, the story of the garden owner mentions two gardens: 'We made for one of them two gardens of grapevines and bordered them with palm trees' (Al-Kahf: 32). Under that verse, we wrote: the mention of two gardens is to show the completion of favor—stating: Allah had given him two gardens out of His favor, but this became a cause of ungratefulness for him.

(A point of eloquence). After this is that verse of repetition, and it will occur after every single blessing. Its context of eloquence is: those deniers who are addressed considered the Resurrection highly improbable, and even if they accepted it, they were not ready to accept that any share in its blessings would belong to these poor Muslims. Their thought was: if the Hereafter is real, they, who received all blessings in the world, would receive them there. To crush this pride of theirs, He mentioned every single blessing of Paradise to state that We will give these to Our pious servants, and asked: 'So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny!' Here you say these are mere fables, but what will you do when We give these to Our servants, and you regret!

"Having spreading branches - So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (48-49)

This describes the fertility and lushness of these two gardens—showing: they will not be barren, but every single tree will be rich in branches and twigs. After this is that verse of repetition, because for the Arabs, gardens were their primary source of pride, and having such lush gardens in a dry country is the height of prosperity. Keeping this taste of theirs in view, He stated: We will give such gardens to Our close servants, so which of His favors would you deny.

"In both of them are two springs, flowing. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (50-51) To guarantee their fertility, two springs will flow in them. So which of your Lord's favors would you deny! After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

"In both of them of every fruit are two kinds. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (52-53) That is: both gardens will not have only one kind of fruit, but both will have different kinds. 'Zawj' (kind) is used in a specific meaning. After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

"[They are] reclining on beds whose linings are of silk brocade, and the fruit of the two gardens is hanging low. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (54-55) That is: the people of Paradise will sit reclining on beds whose linings are of silk brocade. The meaning is: whose linings are of silk brocade, who can imagine their outer covering! Moreover, the fruits of both gardens will be hanging low, and taking them will not require any effort. After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

"In them are women limiting [their] glances, whom no man has touched before them nor jinn - So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny? As if they were rubies and coral. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (56-59) In 'In them' (feehinna), the referent of the plural pronoun are those blessings mentioned above, and also those lawns and places of Paradise which, though not mentioned in words, are understood as necessary attachments. This usage of pronouns is well-known in Arabic. It has passed. It states: in those gardens and provisions will be modest and bashful beauties. Man is made such that he cannot enjoy blessings fully without his wife, whom nature has made his companion; therefore, He stated: they will be highly modest. This modesty is her greatest beauty and the greatest protector of her morals. The woman who is not modest is unfaithful; she cannot be loyal, and when she is not loyal, her existence is without purpose, because she cannot serve as the foundation of a family, whereas this is the objective of her existence. 'whom no man has touched before them nor jinn' is an expression of their untouched nature and virginity. Their comparison with rubies and coral is to describe their beauty and their being protected. After these attributes is that verse of repetition, and its context is clear.

"Is the reward for goodness [anything] but goodness? So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (60-61)

(The testimony of reason and nature for reward and punishment). After describing the reward of the close ones, this addresses the addressees to state a highly eloquent point: why are you surprised that Allah Almighty will give these blessings to His good servants! What else should be the reward of goodness except His favor and bounty? If the Creator did not bring a Day to punish the wicked and reward the righteous, it would mean He has no distinction between goodness and evil; rather, it would mean He approves of evil, as He has given the wicked all resources and left them free to create corruption. Human reason and nature can never tolerate such a bad thought regarding their Lord. The testimony of nature is that the Creator is highly Just and Merciful. He will punish those who commit evil, and reward the righteous. Since this is rooted in human nature, no wise person can deny it; therefore, it is asked in this style. 'Ihsan' means goodness, and also its good reward; here, it is used beautifully in both meanings. After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is: since this is true, Allah will necessarily reward every goodness, so which of His blessings would you deny!

"And below them are two [other] gardens - So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (62-63)

(The other two gardens). Besides those two gardens, this mentions two other gardens which share some characteristics with those, but are different in some aspects. The question arises: will those deserving of these two types of gardens be the same or different? The correct answer will follow in the subsequent Surah, Al-Waqi'ah. It divides the believers into two groups: the companions of the right, and the foremost; and describes different gardens for both, which share some attributes and differ in some. Therefore, the plausible view is: above, the close ones (the foremost) were mentioned, and now, those below them (the companions of the right) are mentioned. Obviously, just as their ranks differ, their gardens differ, as those of taste can estimate. Below, we will also point to some differences. 'Min doonihima' (below them/other than them) can mean other than them, or lower than them. After this is that verse of repetition; its meaning is: there are two other gardens, so which of His gardens would you deny and mock!

"Dark green [in color] - So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (64-65) That is: their lushness will be such that they appear blackish. This is the most beautiful color of a lush garden. After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

"In both of them are two springs, gushing forth. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (66-67) 'Nadh-dhakhatan' means gushing and boiling forth. This is a depiction of mountain springs. The springs of the valleys, as has passed in 'flowing' (tajriyan) above, only flow; but mountain springs gush forth. After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

"In both of them are fruit and palm trees and pomegranates. So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?" (67-69) The mention of 'palm trees' (nakhl) and 'pomegranates' (rumman) after 'fruit' (fakihatun) is the specific after the general, and these two were the beloved fruits of the Arabs. In the preceding Paradise, it was: 'of every fruit are two kinds.' Keep the difference in view. After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

'Khayratun hisan' means those of pure character and pure beauty. Within 'fair women kept close in pavilions' (Hoorun maqsooratun fil-khiyam), the specific taste of the Arabs is prominent: living in tents was the beloved living of the Arabs. The tents and canopies of the Arab chiefs were worthy of envy even for magnificent palaces, and here, the mention is of the tents of Paradise. In the preceding Paradise, the modest beauties were mentioned and compared with rubies and coral. Keep the difference in view. After every single attribute, the verse of repetition has occurred, and its context is entirely clear.

That is: they will sit reclining on green cushions and beautiful, precious carpets. 'Abqariy' is used for rare and precious things, and in this context, we have taken it in the sense of precious carpets. After this is that verse of repetition, and its context is entirely clear.

"Blessed is the name of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor." (78)

(Conclusion of the Surah). Just as after describing the proofs, He stated: 'Everyone upon the earth will perish, And there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor,' similarly, after describing the details of the reward, He repeated this, and the objective is to show: since the Essence of your Lord, the Owner of Majesty and Honor, is highly Blessed, His blessings will necessarily appear. Foolish indeed are those who think that He created this world but has no concern with its good and evil. Just as in the preceding verse, 'Face' (wajh) was used, in this verse, 'Name' (ism) is used to refer to His essence. The name is proof of the named.

Reflecting on these descriptions of Paradise, it is clearly felt: in the preceding Paradise, the taste of the Persians is more prominent, and in the subsequent Paradise, the taste of the Arabs is more prominent. Although these are described as comparisons, and their true reality is known only to Allah, yet with their help, we can form a general idea of them, and this is what is intended. Praise belongs to Allah that today, 24 Ramadan, 1397 AH, the commentary of this Surah is completed. Praise belongs to Allah for His favor. Lahore. 10 September, 1977 AD. 24 Ramadan, 1397 AH.
